title: A Journey into Covenant Theology - Part 3
speaker: Mr. Isaiah Couch
source_files: A Journey into Covenant Theology - Part 3 - Mr. Isaiah Couch.txt

outline.title: Historical Origin of the Covenant of Works
subpoints: The title first appears in the late 16th century (Dudley Fenner 1585, William Perkins 1591)
Zacharias Ursinus (Heidelberg Catechism author) distinguished covenant of works from covenant of grace in his Larger Catechism (1561)
Martin Luther in his Romans commentary used covenantal terms to distinguish Adam's imputed unrighteousness from Christ's imputed righteousness
Augustine affirmed that Adam's perfect obedience would have advanced him to eternal life with God

outline.title: Objections and Answers
subpoints: Dispensationalist objection (Charles Ryrie): theological covenants are deductions, not the result of inductive Bible study
The New Testament itself interprets Old Testament types in light of Christ — this is not a Hermeneutical straitjacket
Reformed objection (John Murray): the word 'covenant' (berit) is not used with Adam, so Murray preferred 'Adamic administration'
Yet Murray himself lists all the qualifications of a covenant relationship when describing the Adamic administration

outline.title: The Covenant of Works Defined and Located in Scripture
subpoints: Westminster Confession Chapter 7: God condescends to express his will by way of covenant; life promised to Adam on condition of perfect obedience
Distinction between Adam's natural obedience (Gen. 2:7-9) and covenantal obedience with terms, conditions, and sanctions (Gen. 2:15-17)
Voss's concept of 'indefinite probation': Adam as creature had no inherent claim to eternal life; only God's voluntary condescension could offer it
Tipton: the probationary tree told Adam that man does not live by bread alone but by every word from God's mouth

outline.title: Adam's Violation and the Weight of the Fall
subpoints: Adam as federal/covenantal head: his guilt is imputed to all descendants by ordinary generation
The covenant of works, once promising life, now produces only death for all under Adam's headship
Westminster Confession Chapter 6: by Adam's sin, all became dead in sin and wholly defiled in soul and body
Understanding federal headship gives proper weight to the catastrophe of the Fall

outline.title: Christ as the True and Better Adam
subpoints: Luke's baptism and genealogy present Christ as the promised seed of the woman who will crush the serpent's head
Christ's wilderness temptation (Luke 4) is a recapitulation of Adam's probationary test — but Christ obeys where Adam failed
The threefold temptation is repeated at the cross (Luke 23:35-39) and Christ remains faithful
Through his perfect obedience Christ advances from probationary life to consummated life; his righteousness is imputed to the elect

summary:
In the third part of his series, Mr. Isaiah Couch turns to the Covenant of Works — the one he confesses wrestled with the most in his Reformed journey. Coming out of dispensationalism and Arminianism, he initially believed the doctrine was absent from Scripture and made God's relationship with Adam purely transactional and loveless. This lecture traces how those objections dissolved as he worked through the writings of covenant theologians and examined Scripture afresh.

Couch establishes that while the title 'Covenant of Works' appears in the late 16th century, the substance of the doctrine is found in Ursinus, Luther, and Augustine. He answers the dispensationalist objection (Ryrie) that covenant theology forces the New Testament back into the Old by pointing out that the New Testament itself does exactly this — interpreting Adam, Moses, and David as types fulfilled in Christ. He also engages John Murray's Reformed objection, showing that Murray's own description of the 'Adamic administration' contains all the hallmarks of a covenant.

The lecture's theological center is the distinction between Adam's natural state as a creaturely being (Genesis 2:7-9) and the covenant relationship God graciously condescended to establish with him (Genesis 2:15-17). Drawing on Voss and Tipton, Couch explains that Adam had no inherent claim to eternal life — God voluntarily offered it on the condition of obedience. Far from being loveless, this was an act of extraordinary divine condescension. The probationary tree, Tipton argues, was God's invitation to Adam to live by every word from his mouth rather than by bread alone.

The climax of the lecture is the contrast between the two federal heads. Adam's violation plunged all humanity into death and guilt. But Christ, the true and better Adam, recapitulates and reverses Adam's failure. His wilderness temptation mirrors Adam's garden test, and the very temptations hurled at him on the cross echo Satan's assault in Eden. Christ remains obedient in every test, advances from probationary life to consummated life, and his perfect covenant-keeping is imputed to all who are united to him by faith.
transcript:
Well, good morning again everybody. First I would like to thank once again Pastor T2 and the rest of the session and allowing me to continue to speak about my journey into the covenant theology. I previously shared about the covenant of redemption last month. This morning I'll be speaking about the covenant of works. And then in July I will be speaking on the covenant of grace. And so of the three foundational covenants within covenant theology, redemption, works, and grace, the covenant of works was the first that I was introduced to in my own journey. In coming from a non-reformed understanding of Scripture, and with my recent departure from Arminianism and dispensationalism, still very fresh in my mind, the covenant of works was the one that I objected to the most. I wrestled with this what felt like a lifetime. But was to speak of a covenant between God and Adam in the Garden, with terms and conditions, I believed as I did with the covenant of redemption, not only extended beyond what was written in Scripture, but was absent from Scripture entirely. I believed that this covenant removed all love between God and His creation and made it purely a transactional relationship where God owed Adam eternal life based upon his obedience.

So this morning I want to focus on, similar to what we did with the covenant of redemption, my approach will be the same. But as I was working through this covenant and reading different writers, different covenant theologians such as O. Palmer Robertson, R.C. Sproul, Geerhardus Vos, and a more modern theologian Lane Tipton who is an OPC pastor, this covenant that I had wrestled with and objected to the most in my journey became a beautiful doctrine, which I saw Scripture declaring of the true and better Adam as our federal covenantal head.

## Historical Origin and Development

As with the covenant of redemption, the term covenant of works was not used until the title was not used until 1638 at the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland by a pastor David Dixon. This in no way, we saw, should be an argument for the validity of the covenant. It should not be based upon when was the title used. We saw that the doctrine itself was taught throughout Church history. Dr. Chad Van Dixhoorn recently — he gave the table lectures this past year. He says, if you spot four English ladies sipping tea, eating scones, and savoring the local gossip at four o'clock in the afternoon, you do not need a label to know what is going on. There is a tea party.

With regard to the historical origin of its title, the covenant of works is believed to first be seen in the late 16th century in the writings of Dudley Fenner, Sacred Theology 1585, and William Perkins, Golden Chain 1591. But while the exact origin of the title is hard to pinpoint, the doctrine is there. It was taught within the church. Zacharias Ursinus, who was one of the most influential theologians in writing the Heidelberg Catechism, in his Larger Catechism written in 1561, explains the distinction between the covenant of works and the covenant of grace. In question and answer 10, Ursinus writes, Question: what does the divine law teach? Answer: it teaches the kind of covenant God established with man in creation. How man behaved in keeping it and what God requires of him after establishing the new covenant of grace with him.

Ursinus saw the first covenant that God entered into with Adam at creation was different from the covenant of grace that God established with him after Adam's covenant violation so that he may be reconciled to God. The term covenant of works is not used by Ursinus, but the language is. Before Ursinus, Martin Luther in his commentary on Paul's letter to the Romans, specifically on chapter 5, which we will look at later, used covenantal terms when distinguishing between all of humanity receiving the imputed unrighteousness of Adam as the federal or covenantal head for humanity, and those who are regenerated receiving the imputed righteousness of Christ as the covenantal head of those chosen by God from before the foundation of the world. Luther says in his commentary, as Adam became a cause of death to his descendants, though they did not eat of the forbidden tree, so Christ has become the dispenser of righteousness to those who are of him, though they have not earned any righteousness, for through the cross he has secured righteousness for them.

Augustine in the City of God clearly recognizes that in the garden, if Adam would live a life of perfect obedience to the commands of God, he would enter into eternal life and perfect fellowship and communion with God. Augustine says, man on the other hand God created in such sort that if he remained in subjection to his creator as his rightful Lord, and piously kept his commandments, he should pass into the company of the angels and obtain without the intervention of death a blessed and endless immortality.

## Objections and Answers

Now while I in my own journey may rejoice now in being able to share these different theologians who have seen this doctrine as being taught by scripture, in my own journey it was not always the case. In coming from a non-reformed understanding of scripture, I struggled in embracing this understanding of covenant between God and Adam in the garden. Typically, in coming from dispensationalism and having a literalistic, bibliocistic understanding of scripture — a chapter and verse for everything — if it does not say covenant in the Bible, it is not a covenant. That was my understanding of things. And I wholeheartedly agreed with a man prominent in dispensationalism, Charles Ryrie. In his book Dispensationalism, he objects to covenant theology by saying, the theological covenants on which covenant theology is based are not specifically revealed in scripture. Other covenants such as the Abrahamic and Davidic are specifically revealed and in great detail, but the all-embracing covenants of covenant theology are not in the Bible. The whole covenant system is based on a deduction, not the result of an inductive study of scripture.

But as I continued to study this topic, read scripture and these different writers, I saw that Ryrie's objection to the covenant theologians' belief being based upon, as he says, a deduction and not the result of an inductive reading of scripture, I found this to be completely backwards. These different theologians were not reading covenant language into scripture, but rather letting scripture speak covenant language to the reader. Ryrie also claims that covenant theology forces the New Testament back into the Old. He says, the Hermeneutical straitjacket that covenant theology forces on the scriptures results in reading the New Testament back into the Old and in an artificial typological interpretation.

Again, the problem I came to see with Ryrie's objection here is that the New Testament itself specifically interprets what we call types and shadows in the Old Testament in light of the New. Since the Old Testament and New Testament have the same author being the Spirit, it is not a straitjacket to bring the New back into the Old or to have the New shed light on the Old. Christ as the true and better Moses as mediator, the true and better David as King, the true and better Melchizedek as great high priest — these are typological passages that the New Testament shows fulfilled in Christ. One typological passage that relates to our topic this morning that we saw Luther commenting on in Romans 5 is where the New Testament declares that Christ is the true and better Adam.

While I know that Charles Ryrie is in no way reformed, that is where I came from. That is what I held to in my dispensational beliefs. And so I wanted to address that. But as I continued to study different objections, I came across some reformed theologians who had reservations and objections to this covenant of works. These objections were from men like Karl Barth and specifically John Murray. Murray presented that the first use of the term berit or covenant was not used until Noah. You do not see it before with Adam. Therefore Murray presents that the relationship between God and Adam in the garden was not one of covenant. Murray preferred the term Adamic administration when speaking of the pre-fall state of Adam in the garden. He presents all of the qualifications for a covenant relationship though when he talks about it. He presents obedience or disobedience, blessing, rewards, curses, punishments. If you just study Murray's definition of the Adamic administration and not his objection, you would think that he was talking about a covenant relationship with God and Adam.

And so while Murray's objection to the term covenant of works is one that I saw most often from other reformed theologians who objected to this, some preferred the covenant of life because life was promised to Adam upon his perfect obedience. Others used the term covenant of nature or the covenant of creation. The consistent recognition from all of these reformed theologians is that there is a covenant — regardless of its title, there is a covenant relationship between God and Adam in the garden before the fall.

## The Covenant of Works Defined

So with having examined the historical origin and development of this covenant and addressing some of these objections, I would now like to focus on properly defining and understanding the operation and purpose of this covenant. First let us establish a foundational definition from our own confession. The Westminster Confession of Faith chapter 7, articles 1 and 2 says, the distance between God and the creature is so great that although reasonable creatures do owe obedience unto him as their creator, yet they could never have any fruition of him as their blessedness and reward, but by some voluntary condescension on God's part, which he has been pleased to express by way of covenant. 2. The first covenant made with man was a covenant of works, wherein life was promised to Adam and in him to his posterity upon condition of perfect and personal obedience.

In understanding the covenant of works, we must know that Adam's original state in the garden did not have any promise of eternal life. Both Tipton and Vos stress the importance of what they call Adam's natural obedience and Adam's covenantal obedience. Vos uses the term indefinite probation. What he means by this is that as a creature made in the image of God, Genesis 1:26-27, Adam's natural obedience carried with it no promise of eternal life with God in heaven. Tipton says this in his book Foundations of Covenant Theology: Adam by nature was a claimless creature of the dust so that after he had done all that he could and rendered his heart to God in full free and sincere obedience, he would have only done what was required of him. If Adam is to have life, it must come by way of creation. If that life is to be advanced, it must come by way of covenant.

So when does the covenant of works begin? Please turn with me to Genesis 2. Starting in verse 7: *and the Lord God formed man of the dust of the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life and man became a living being. The Lord God planted a garden eastward in Eden and there he put the man whom he had formed and out of the ground the Lord God made every tree grow that is pleasant in the sight and good for food. The tree of life was also in the midst of the garden and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.* Skip down to verse 15: *Then the Lord God took the man and put him in the garden of Eden to tend and keep it and the Lord God commanded the man saying, of every tree of the garden you may freely eat but of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat. For in the day that you eat of it you shall surely die.*

In Genesis 2:7, Tipton and Vos would define this as Adam's natural obedience. Adam is a living creature of the dust made in the image of God but this state held no promise of advancement to eternal life or reward to Adam. Genesis 2:15-17 is where covenant language begins as God condescends to Adam and promises life to him based upon his perfect obedience. Both Tipton and Vos define this section as Adam's covenantal obedience. There are conditions, terms, and punishments for violation of the command given by God to Adam.

From Lane Tipton's book Foundations of Covenant Theology I've included a longer quote in your outline: Man is created and then placed by God in the garden to work and keep it. After being placed there he is commanded by God not to eat of the one tree. The probationary tree is ultimately for the good of Adam, since it was given to him to direct his steps to the highest heavens. If Adam walks in the steps that God has laid before him by keeping his word, he will be led up the mountain of God in Eden to walk with the Lord. From probationary life to consummate life, from worship on earth to worship in heaven. The probation tree tells Adam that man does not live by bread alone but by every word that comes from the mouth of God. When Adam was brought to the tree of knowledge of good and evil, he was made to stare into the very heart of religion itself. True religion is to love and to serve the Triune God not for any reward but for the sake of his glory alone.

And so I said at the beginning of this lesson that I believed this covenant removed all love from the relationship between God and Adam. But how wrong I was in this. God did not owe Adam eternal life. He did not need Adam. As we saw, Adam was a claimless creature of the dust who owed God everything. But God in his great love graciously condescended to Adam and promised him eternal life based upon his love for his Creator. I cannot help but think of David in Psalm 8 when he says, when I consider your heavens the work of your fingers, the moon and the stars which you have ordained, what is man that you are mindful of him? This covenant was a voluntary, condescending act of love by God for the creature made in his image.

## Adam's Violation and the Weight of the Fall

This proper understanding of the covenant of works caused me to see and feel the weight of Adam's violation in the covenant of works that I had never realized before. Our Confession says chapter 6 articles 2 and 3: by this sin they fell from their original righteousness and communion with God, and so became dead in sin and wholly defiled in all the faculties and parts of soul and body. They being the root of all mankind, the guilt of the sin was imputed and the same death in sin and corrupted nature conveyed to all their posterity, descending from them by ordinary generation.

Understanding that Adam is a representative man whose actions have determined the eternal destinies of those whom he represents is essential in understanding the first aspect of this covenant. Under Adam as representative, the covenant of works which once promised eternal life in heaven with God now bears only the stench of death and the sting of death to all those who are under his headship.

## Christ as the True and Better Adam

But when I saw the second aspect of the covenant of works, my experience was similar to what happened to Christian in John Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress. He writes: his burden loosened from off his shoulders and fell from his back and began to tumble. And so continued to do till it came to the mouth of the sepulcher where it fell in and I saw it no more. Then was Christian glad and lightsome and said with a merry heart, he has given me rest by his sorrow and life by his death.

Remember the tears I cried when I realized that Adam is not the only representative man. There are two representative men throughout redemptive history: the first Adam and the true and better Adam. Christ is also a representative man whose actions have determined the eternal destinies of those whom he represents. The seed of the woman promised in Genesis 3:15 is the second and final Adam. He lived a perfect life of obedience to the covenant of works. In the gospel of Luke, the baptism and genealogy of Jesus Christ testifies to the reader that he is the promised seed who will crush the head of the serpent. Luke also presents that Christ, just as Adam, went through a time of probationary testing.

Lane Tipton points out that when Jesus is led by the Spirit out into the wilderness, it is a recapitulation of the probationary testing of Adam in the garden. But there are a few points of difference. While Adam was tested in the garden where life was abundant and filled with substance that would sustain life, Christ is tested in the wilderness — a place far from sustaining life but rather a place where it takes life. Tipton says: why was there this contrast between the first and second Adam? Because it was necessary for the feet of the incarnate Messiah to touch the ground that was cursed on account of the first Adam's sin, Genesis 3:17. Jesus is tempted as the God-man who has come to destroy the works of the devil and to bring his people to where he is going — and that is to paradise above.

In Luke 4:1-13, we can examine this in comparison with the testing of Adam in Genesis 3:1-7. The devil comes to bring into question what God has commanded, promised, warned, and covenanted to both of these representative men. Both Adam and Christ are made to stare into the heart of religion. Both are tested to see if they will serve the Triune God not for any reward but for his sake and glory alone. Where Adam fell because he desired to live by something other than every word that comes from the mouth of God, Christ as the true and better Adam declared to Satan, man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word of God.

Tipton also discusses why the devil in the last temptation in the wilderness seems to shift his focus to Christ's feet. Tipton presents that Christ has put his heel to Satan's head in the first two temptations. Satan knows that his only hope is to misdirect the foot of the serpent crusher who is walking the path of perfect obedience and worship to the commands laid before him. He says: the feet of the Messiah are on Satan's mind because he wants them to be taken off his head and he wants them instead to stumble in sin, disobedience and self-promotion. He does not want the feet of the Messiah to ascend to heaven but to fall to the ground and to be dashed to pieces. Jesus however knew that his feet had come for one purpose: to crush the serpent's head.

The same temptation is repeated to Christ on the cross. Luke 23:35-39: and the people stood looking on, but even the rulers with them sneered, saying, he saved others, let him save himself if he is the Christ, the chosen of God. The soldiers also mocked him, coming and offering him sour wine and saying, if you are the king of the Jews, save yourself. Then one of the criminals who were hanged blasphemed him, saying, if you are the Christ, save yourself and us.

The ridiculing thief on the cross has the same subtle cunningness of what Satan did with Adam and Eve in the garden. The thief cries out, if you are the Christ, save yourself and us. Tipton says: this criminal cunningly appeals to Jesus' messianic self-understanding and asks him to abuse the conception of salvation and to redefine it as a temporal rescue from imminent death. The same appeal is made in the garden to Adam — to redefine and to twist the terms that God has commanded and covenanted to them. But where Adam failed and plunged all of humanity into the state of unrighteousness, Christ obeyed. And through his obedience and faithfulness to the covenant of works, he advances from probationary life to consummated life, from worship on earth to worship in heaven. And his covenant faithfulness and righteousness is imputed to all of those who were chosen from before the foundation of the world in the covenant of redemption.

As our Confession says chapter 8 article 5: the Lord Jesus by his perfect obedience and sacrifice of himself, which he through the eternal Spirit once offered up unto God, have fully satisfied the justice of his Father, and purchased not only reconciliation, but an everlasting inheritance in the kingdom of God, for all those whom the Father has given unto him.

## Application

Enclosing, why is this proper understanding of the covenant of works important? Why does it matter? Because it teaches us that our salvation is entirely based upon the person and work of the true and better Adam. As Horatius Bonar penned in his magnificent hymn: Now what my hands have done can save my guilty soul. Now what my toiling flesh has won can make my spirit whole. Thy work alone, O Christ, can ease this weight of sin. Thy blood alone, O Lamb of God, can give me peace within.

The recognition of our salvation being in Christ alone in both his person and his work should lead us to rejoice in the benefits that are ours in him, especially our justification and sanctification. In the true and better Adam, we stand accepted, vindicated, and given the gift of eternal life in perfect communion with our Creator. The believer can rejoice in having blessed assurance and knowing that Jesus is mine. Confident in what the second Adam has done for us to secure our redemption. Because just as with the covenant of redemption, the work of Christ in the covenant of works cannot be undone. Scripture testifies to this in Philippians 1:6 again — he who has begun the good work in you will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ. Also in John 6:39, when Jesus says that all that he has given to me I should lose nothing but raise it up at the last day.

The assurance and anticipation of the believer is to participate in what Christ has received in his perfect obedience in the covenant of works, that he graciously gives to his people. And that participation is ascending the mountain of God and having perfect fellowship and communion with him for eternity. As 1 Corinthians 15:42-46 says, so also is the resurrection of the dead. The body is sown in corruption, it is raised in incorruption. It is sown in dishonor, it is raised in glory. It is sown in weakness, it is raised in power. It is sown a natural body, it is raised a spiritual body. There is a natural body and there is a spiritual body. And so it is written, the first Adam became a living being. The last Adam became a life-giving spirit.

So what then is to be our response in understanding this covenant? One more quote from Lane Tipton: our response can only be to join our voices with the voices of the Father and the Son and the Spirit and to confess Jesus Christ as the second and last Adam who by his death and resurrection has once for all opened the gates of Paradise for us. What he has opened, no one can close. Therefore abide in him. Rise up into that paradise by faith and worship the Triune God today in the power of the Spirit. As you ascend the heights of Mount Zion and in union with Christ, worship and adore the living and self-contained Triune God forever.

Our Almighty God of Covenant, we thank you that you graciously chose to condescend to us and lovingly offer a claimless creature of the dust a pathway to eternal fellowship with you. We know that you were not required to give us this gift. And while we know that the first Adam failed, we thank you for the second Adam that did not, who obeyed perfectly your commands and the covenant that you made with him. So we rejoice in the work of Christ. We joyfully look forward to the day when Christ returns and together with the saints throughout the ages, whose redemption has been purchased through the blood of Christ, we may ascend the mountain of God to be with you. And I ask you, would you bless the rest of your Lord's day, bless the preaching of your word as your people gather together to worship our covenant-making covenant-keeping God. We ask all these things in the name of your Son through the Holy Spirit. Amen.
